titleStmt.title: Harvesting Textual and Structured Data from the HAL Publication Repository

analytic.title: Harvesting Textual and Structured Data from the HAL Publication Repository

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents

div.p: HAL (Hyper Articles en Ligne) is the French national publication repository, used by most higher education and research organizations for their open science policy. Although it is a rich repository of academic documents, its potential for advanced research has not been fully explored. We present HALvest, a unique dataset that bridges the gap between citation networks and the full text of HAL-submitted articles to help with authorship attribution and verification. This first iteration consists of approximately 700,000 documents, spanning 56 languages across 13 identified domains. We transform articles' metadata into a citation network, producing a heterogeneous graph. This graph includes uniquely identified authors on HAL, as well as all open-access documents and their references. Finally, we mine 14.5 million high-quality sequence pairs from HALvest for contrastive learning purposes. By providing different views of HAL, suited for modern machine learning, we aim to assist practitioners in better analyzing and interpreting research dynamics.

div.head: Domain (16)
div.p: Has Topic (136,700) Paper (18,662,037) Affiliated with (426,030) Writes (834,644

div.head: Introduction
div.p: Publication repositories are the norm for openly storing and distributing scholarly papers. When submitting a paper to a repository, the depositor, an identified user, possesses a unique id in the repository's database. However, the depositor's coauthors might not be identified users, letting the repository perform id retrieval to map the submission to the correct authors. Authorship attribution without human intervention remains a non-trivial task for publication repositories (Tekles and Bornmann, 2019). Researchers quickly appreciated the importance of coauthorship in tackling authorship attribution, hence integrating structured data into their proposed solutions (Shin et al., 2014;Ma et al., 2020;Xie et al., 2022). However, the shortcomings of graph-based algorithms, in the absence of The number of nodes or edges of a given type is given in the parenthesis). co-authorship, drove researchers to exploit semantic and symbolic features from papers (Tran et al., 2014;Müller, 2017;Kim et al., 2019;Cohan et al., 2020;Boukhers et al., 2021;Pooja et al., 2021;Kojaku et al., 2021) and investigating the semantic fingerprint from individuals (Han et al., 2017;Zhai et al., 2019).

Recently, with research in multimodal deep learning mushrooming, several architectures allow practitioners to benefit from text and structured data (Zhang et al., 2019(Zhang et al., , 2022;;Yasunaga et al., 2022), thus exploiting citation networks and semantic content from scholarly articles (Pooja et al., 2022;Santini et al., 2022). Another key factor in tackling authorship attribution is the increasing capacity of language models to encode stylometric features (Fabien et al., 2020;Ai et al., 2022;Wegmann et al., 2022). By opening the data from HAL (Hyper Articles en Ligne), we would enable researchers to implement and validate new methods towards better authorship attribution or verification, by exploiting different aspects inherent to publication repositories. We introduce HALvest, a structured and textual dataset: the structured part,

div.head: Dataset
div.p: References Full-text Multilingual Multi-domain CURATED GRAPH DATASETS ArnetMiner (Tang et al., 2012) ✓ LARGE GRAPH DATASETS MAG (Wang et al., 2020) ✓ ✓ OpenAlex (Priem et al., 2022) called HALvest-Geometric, is a heterogeneous citation network comprising 238,397 author nodes and 18,662,037 paper nodes, with 642,723 of them having their full-text available: although fuzzy, this heterogeneous graph still provides useful information about citation and collaboration dynamics. In addition, the gathered papers allow for more than 16.5 billion tokens from scholarly text-Note that it is a coarse approximation as some of the data may not be clean. Finally, we derived another dataset from the textual data: HALvest-Contrastive, intending to perform contrastive learning at different levels on sentence pairs, and hopefully, better understand how language models embed stylometry.

To validate HALvest's suitability to not only train language models but also graph neural networks (GNN), we conduct a straightforward experiment: we test several GNN (Scarselli et al., 2009) to provide a baseline for authorship attribution in a closed set-up, while also validating our graph's format.
Our contribution can be summarized as follows
• A main, unfiltered, textual dataset, comprising 17 billion tokens in 56 languages and 13 domains.
• An academic citation network with 238,397 disambiguated authors and 18,662,037 scholarly papers.
• A supplementary, cleaned, contrastive learning dataset derived from the main dataset.
The original dataset 1 as well as the geometric 2 , and the contrastive 3 datasets can be found online. We also open the code used to craft them 4 .

div.head: Related Work
div.p: Scholarly textual data The growing use of language modeling in natural language processing, along with the central role of online publication repositories, has led to the release of a significant amount of textual data. Although full-texts are not bound to the same licensing as abstracts, publication libraries in science, technology, engineering, and math are often at the forefront when it comes to opening their data when possible. The likes of ArXiv 5 and DBLP 6 PubMed 7 are allowing their publication's titles and abstract to be crawled (arXiv.org submitters, 2024;Wahle et al., 2022;Sen et al., 2008;Dogan et al., 2014) while Semantic Scholar 8 and a subset of PubMed (PMC) 9 , allow for a full-texts' crawling (Ammar et al., 2018;Lee et al., 2020) of their data. ACL 10 , on the other hand, opted for a cleaned subset of 10,920 academic papers from ACL Anthology (Bird et al., 2008). However, the aforementioned repositories are either limited in size or domains (e.g., STEM for ArXiv, computer science for DBLP, and bio-medical for PubMed), hence Lo et al. (2020) merged various sources in order to alleviate these issues, while also offering full texts from open-access papers. Academic unstructured data is also built and maintained by researchers' communities as seen in BC5CDR (Li et al., 2016) and NCBI (Dogan et al., 2014), andMEDLINE (Vishnyakova et al., 2019) in the biomedical domain. All the aforementioned datasets are confined to the English language and featured publications or preprints, while the unstructured half of HALvest provides historical texts, thesis, and dictionaries, for example, being more lengthy and domaindiverse. Besides, the full texts are uniform in their formatting and referencing style.

Authored sentence pairs The literature on authorship attribution often involves setting up a closed environment with a finite list of authors and documents, before performing classification-may it be binary or not. To this end, they rely on lowentropy text from online forums or open-domain sources (Schler et al., 2006;Seroussi et al., 2014). Although Wegmann and Nguyen (2021) propose a dataset decorrelating the writing style of a document from its content, it remains within a lowentropy setup, with minimal token overlap.

Academic graph When it comes to academic graph, ArnetMiner (Tang et al., 2008(Tang et al., , 2012) ) and Microsoft Academic Graph (MAG) (Wang et al., 2020) are often the go-to, comprising of metadata from DBLP and ACM 11 . MAG, unlike Ar-netMiner, has not been manually curated but allows for a rich pool of papers and authors (736,389 papers, 1,134,649 authors 8,740 institutions, and 59,965 fields of study nodes). One can also look upon OpenAlex (Priem et al., 2022), offering more than 220 million publications nodes, and the graph proposed by (Ammar et al., 2018) to enable semantic features in Semantic Scholar. All the graphs mentioned above come with various metadata, including abstracts from articles.

div.head: Combined Text and academic knowledge graph
div.p: To the best of our knowledge, the only ready-to-use academic graph mapped with full texts comes from OpenAlex (Priem et al., 2022). While, ACL (Cohan et al., 2019), and Lee et al. (2020)'s datasets can be easily mapped to citation networks with little processing, they proved to be limited in size and domain (computational linguistic and biomedical respectively).

div.head: Collection Methodology
div.p: 3.1 Fetching data from HAL HAL's API allows for precise queries to the repository, enabling the retrieval of XML-TEI responses for each submitted document, thereby acquiring all available metadata in a structured format ( § B).

11 https://dl.acm.org/ We design our query to only fetch submissions with an attached document in open access, and apply further filtering to only fetch work submitted alongside a PDF file. As of February 2024, the total number of open submitted PDF files is 778,072.

We use GROBID (GROBID Repository) to derive XML files from PDFs, hence easing the conversion to plain text afterward. Reference markers in each document are serialized following the scheme defined by Taylor et al. (2022). However, math, SMILES formulas, as well as code, are encoded in plain utf-8 and left as is.

Why using GROBID Relying on GROBID as a proxy before performing PDF parsing may seem far-fetched, as easier and more accurate ways exist to extract text from PDFs-think of visionlanguage models and Optical Character Recognition (OCR). However, having a latent structured view of our articles will enable finer control over the desired text output we want in the future. The long-term goal is to be able to standardize and change the format of high-bandwidth text from research papers at will (citations, formulas, code...).

div.head: Filtering
div.p: During the conversion process, if the fonts within a PDF lack Unicode tables and do not employ standard encoding for mapping glyph indices to characters, GROBID's output ends up being gibberish ( §A.2), as the latter do not employ optical character recognition to extract text from PDFs, but rather its layout. To filter out gibberish documents, we use a set of heuristic functions ( §A.3). Following Raffel et al. ( 2020 2023), we compute a set of statistics about each document, effectively getting metrics like the number of lines, the average word length or the ratio of unique words. We use the implementation provided by RedPajama Repository. To compute the ratio of stop-words in a document, we use stopwords-json (6, 2024).

div.head: Contrastive pairs
div.p: Performing authorship attribution, verification, or stylometry embedding analysis involves performing classification on authored passages. We perform passage mining to derive a dataset of contrastive pairs from HAL. We only consider documents with at least one identified author citing other articles, as it is a proxy to identify research papers. To facilitate future studies, we filter out documents addressing subjects that are not within the 13 identified domains.

For each document, we sample 4 raw positive pairs and 4 raw negative ones. We use two straightforward algorithms to mine sentence pairs. The first one consists of independently sampled sentences from the same first author for positive pairs and mutually exclusive sets of authors for negative ones. We also derive dataset split using this algorithm, where sentences written by at least one mutual author suffice to form a positive pair.

Independent sampling For each document D we sample 8 n-sized queries q, where the size denotes the number of contiguous sentences. The second step is to sample 4 n-sized positive passages p + from the same first author and 4 negative passages p -with no mutual coauthors to ensure a minimum difference in writing style. If a given first author doesn't have any other entry in the dataset, we sample p + from the same document D.

div.head: Inverse cloze task (ICT)
div.p: In the inverse cloze task (ICT) setting (Lee et al., 2019;Izacard et al., 2022), we identify a set of N = n * 3 contiguous sentences S. We concatenate

to form the passage context p + to retrieve. We use the remaining [[s N/3 , s 2N/3 ]] as a query q. More formally, for S = {s 1 , s 2 , ..., s a , ..., s b , ..., s n * 3 }, we have q = {s a , ..., s b } and p + = {s 1 , s 2 , ..., s a-1 , s b+1 , ..., s n * 3 }.
After sampling enough sentences, we postprocess the newly obtained pairs to remove symbol and math-heavy strings, repetitive sequences with unigram entropy, and sequences with less than 4 words.

div.head: From metadata to citation network
div.p: Building the citation network is straightforward. We define four node types, and four edge types, before iterating through the metadata of each document.

HAL offers a tree-like domain structure consisting of 13 main domains, each branching into several subdomains, along with an additional category: 'Other.' We retrieve the domains assigned by the depositors but only consider the root node for our analysis. It is worth noting that HAL provides mirrors for documents hosted on other platforms, each with its own domain space.

Computing the citations is a fuzzy process: we retrieve each document's references through their GROBID's XML view, and fetch their title and publication year, before performing an exact matching and enriching the graph. This process can induce inaccuracies in the title retrieved, as GROBID parsing can be inexact in its processing.

div.head: Unstructured data
div.p: Although HALvest is mostly in English and French, the gathered 670,861 papers are written in 56 languages across 16 domains, accounting for approximately 17 billion tokens. HALvest's text can also serve as a valuable asset for low-resource languages, hosting documents in Basque, Catalan, or Persian to mention a few ( §B.1).

As for the contrastive part, we compute ten different English splits. For each split, we vary the number of sentences in both the query q and the passage p with n = {2, 4, 6, 8}. The first four splits are independently sampled pairs where we only consider the first author to verify p + , while we consider any of the coauthors in the next four ones. The two remaining splits ICT pairs with n = 2 and n = 4. We mine more than 14.5 million sequence pairs this way.

div.head: Structured Data
div.p: HALvest-Geometric is made up of a heterogeneous graph. Following Wang et al. (2020), we compute 238,397 author nodes, 18,662,037 paper nodes, 96,105 institution nodes, and 16 domain nodes for a total of18,996,55 nodes as of February 2024. We also define 4 different edge types, constituting 23,761,191 edges.

div.head: Do citations help, even when fuzzy?
div.p: In this subsection, we will evaluate the citations extracted from each publication, as described in subsection 3.4. Because we fuzzily retrieve the citations, the added information to the citation network can be nothing more than noise. A straightforward way to estimate the usefulness of this retrieved information is to use graph neural networks (GNN) and message passing. Message passing allows GNNs to discover the graph's structure from the way information propagates on it. Another key concept to better understand our experiment is a property called homophily: adjacent nodes should have similar features; in our context, since authors would most likely cite people from their fields, the assumption that our data is highly homophilic is not far-fetched. Therefore, the subsequent graph neural networks used to represent our graph should perform better in setting with domain-related papers as adjacent nodes, than with other papers two hopes away, bridged by a domain node.

div.head: Tasks & Settings
div.p: Authorship attribution Given a document d ∈ D and a candidate author a ∈ A, we want to compute a probability
that the individual a is an author of d.

Link prediction When given a citation network, the authorship attribution problem can be reformulated as a link prediction problem. Given a graph G(V, E) with V the set of all nodes and E ⊆ |V| × |V| the set of all the true edges in G. In our setting, given a potential author node u and a paper node v, we want to learn a classifier that predicts the probability of the existence of an edge, by computing a score between the representations of both incident nodes ŷu∼v = f (h u , h v ) where h u and h v are learned representation of said nodes. In this task we use a set of candidate edges E ′ comprising of positive and negative edges between the incident nodes, allowing us to use a binary cross entropy loss.
This closed setting, however, doesn't account for unknown authors, as we only consider identified candidates from the citation network to perform link prediction.

Baselines HALvest's heterogeneous graph is featureless, prompting us to learn a representation for each node. We use several GNN architectures (Scarselli et al., 2009) to learn embeddings for each node while training.

Evaluation We report the area under the curve (AUC) as a measure of the quality of a link prediction algorithm. Since our experiment is done in a closed setting, we compute five random splits of the original citation network, keeping 10% of the author ↔ paper edges for validation and 20% for test purposes. Table 2: Area under the curve (with standard deviation) of each link prediction model. The result is the average performance of 5 models trained with 5 different random seeds. We use GraphSage (Hamilton et al., 2017), graph attention network (Veličković et al., 2018) and residual gated graph convolutions (Bresson and Laurent, 2018).

div.head: Link Prediction
div.p: We build a simple link prediction architecture, comprising of an embedding layer for each node typeof dimension R |V ′ |×16 where |V ′ | is the number of nodes of a given type-followed by two GNN layers, to learn an inductive 16 dimensions representation of each node. The implemented GNNs are GraphSAGE (Hamilton et al., 2017), a graph attention network (Veličković et al., 2018), and a residual gated graph convolutional network (Bresson and Laurent, 2018). Finally, we compute the cosine similarity between the candidate author and paper nodes, map it to probabilities, and determine the presence or absence of a link between each pair of nodes. We follow (Fey and Lenssen, 2019)'s implementation and perform Bayesian optimization to find the best hyperparameters (Li et al., 2020;Bergstra et al., 2015). We report the training results in Table 2. The training hyperparameters and learning metrics can be found in §C.1.

div.head: Results
div.p: The results of our experiments provide compelling evidence that incorporating citations, even when retrieved fuzzily, enhances the performance of graphbased models in authorship verification tasks. The AUC scores, as presented in Table 2, illustrate this improvement across various GNN architectures. Including fuzzy citations provides a useful signal, improving the performance on authorship verification by approximately 10% across all models.

The comparative analysis of different GNN architectures further validates our approach. All three models-GraphSAGE, GAT, and RGGCexhibited significant performance drops when citation information was removed. This consistency across multiple architectures suggests that the benefits of incorporating citations are not model-specific but rather a generalizable advantage. Moreover, the standard deviations reported alongside the AUC scores reflect the robustness of our models.

div.head: Limitations
div.p: PDF processing Solely relying on GROBID to process the PDFs hindered the dataset creation in some aspects. As only the PDF's layout tokens matter to GROBID, and no optical character recognition is performed, documents with odd layouts cannot be effectively used after conversion, and are outright discarded. Most of the documents are seamlessly converted, yet some of them contain spans of text that are nearly gibberish, embedded within otherwise clean sentences. We designed our filtering process to remove documents with a prior unusable layout, but not to remove the gibberish span of text within clean data. Therefore, further processing effort is needed from practitioners. As a rough estimation, Antoun et al. (2024) preprocessed HALvest's French split at a sentence level, removing tabs and some math formulas, and ended with about 52% of the total tokens (4.7 billion tokens). But this estimation can vary from one language to another, as GROBID to not process Arabic documents correctly, for example, reversing every word.

Multilingual documents Some documents are written in two or more languages, but HAL requires the depositor to specify only one language. It is therefore necessary to perform language identification at the sentence level to ensure only the needed language is fetched from the dataset.

Accounting for references When building the citation network, the references at the end of each paper are not consolidated, resulting in a fuzzy process of deduplication afterward. This results in inaccuracies when it comes to modeling citations.

div.head: Conclusion
div.p: By processing approximately 700,000 documents in 56 languages across 13 domains of HAL, we have created a unique dataset, mapping natural language to a heterogeneous graph. This first iteration of HALvest includes 16.5 billion tokens for 18,996,555 graph nodes, enabling extensive research in multimodality, authorship attribution, domain classification, and more. In addition, we provide a ready-to-use contrastive learning dataset with 14.5 million rows in total.

We elaborate on the usefulness of HALvest, by performing authorship verification, using several GNN architectures, and discuss the added value of retrieved citations, further confirming HALvest's adequacy for scholarly article analysis.

Future work will focus on expanding the dataset and improving the different preprocessing pipelines. A proper stylometry-based authorship verification will also be conducted, using the contrastive learning dataset.
The datasets, along with the associated code, are openly available.

div.head: Ethics Statement
div.p: In this work, we have taken careful measures to ensure that our dataset is constructed solely from open-access scholarly documents available in the HAL repository. All source materials are distributed under open licenses, and our data collection pipeline strictly adheres to the usage terms provided by HAL.

Furthermore, our processing workflow-ranging from PDF-to-text conversion with GROBID to subsequent filtering-has been designed to preserve the integrity of the original texts while minimizing the risk of propagating errors. The dataset is intended solely for research purposes, and does not include any sensitive personal information beyond what is publicly accessible.

div.head: A Collection Methodology
div.formula: F F F F F F F F F F F F F F F F F I IFIFI ystem model F F F F F F F F F F F F F F F F F F F F F F F I IFIFP vrge dimensionl regime F F F F F F F F F... 00177057 , 4 @ / A -@ & $ B 4 & A / 2 @ - " 0 / -4 , / 3 $C" ' 5$ B 5C % " +0 2 C % BB * ( ( $ -$ @" ' + 0 D 0 E $ F $&$@" C % < = $ B 7 (#7 $ -$ @" 0 D 0 E @" @ C G $ ( > (( < 5HI 97>89 D (0 E " @C"@F C G $.

div.head: A.3 Document Level Filtering
div.p: We post-process the remaining documents, written in 34 languages, as follows:
1. Documents with less than 3 words are discarded as they likely do not provide any valuable information.

2. Documents with more than 10% of words that are capitalized are discarded. Some gibberish documents consist of random capitalized letters ( §A.2). This threshold is low to not filter out documents with a lot of DNA sequences or a lot of tables with capitalized sections.
3. Documents with more than 60% of words that are not alphanumeric are discarded. Some gibberish documents consist of random words with non-alphanumeric characters overrepresented ( §A.2).
4. Documents with an average word length of 1.5 characters or below are discarded. GROBID sometimes escapes the words in a document letter by letter ( §A.2).

5. Document with no stop words are discarded. Stop words are strong indicators for wellredacted documents, hence, lowering the chance of it being gibberish. Besides, we use stop words as a language identifier, as the language provided in the metadata is specified by a depositor, and human error can be introduced.

6. We compute the inverse fertility (Rust et al., 2021): the number of words in a document divided by the number of tokens (we use mT5 (Xue et al., 2021) for tokenization). After removing special tokens, a tokenizer yields at most an amount of tokens equal to the number of words, effectively bounding our function between 0 and 1. An inverse fertility score close to zero is a strong indication of over tokenization, and therefore, hinting at a gibberish document. Documents with an inverse fertility score lower than 0.2 are discarded.

div.head: B Composition
div.p: We selected eight features to represent the submissions:
• halid: submission's unique identifier assigned by HAL.
• lang: the language of the document, as filled by the depositor.

figure.head: Figure 1 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 1: HALvest's citation network: a directed heterogeneous graph with 4 node types and 4 edge types.The number of nodes or edges of a given type is given in the parenthesis).
);Wenzek et al. (2020);Rae et al. (2022) andPenedo et al. (

figure.head: Table 1 :
figure.figDesc: Information provided by HALvest compared to previous academic graph datasets. References denote the presence of a citation list for a given paper.

figure.head: Table 3 :
figure.figDesc: Examples of halid and output text from PDF files with no Unicode mapping, leading to gibberish text. While the first two examples are from discarded documents, the third one is kept, as only the section names are not processed correctly.

div.head: Acknowledgements
div.p: The authors are grateful to the CCSD staff, Achraf Azhar, and Patrice Lopez for providing resources and support to better handle HAL and GROBID. We also thank Arij, Riabi, Brahim Talb and Menel Mahamdi for the productive discussions.
• title Title of the document.
• domain: list of field of studies 12 .
• timestamp: time of access.
• year: publication year of the document if relevant. Otherwise, it is set to year 1.
• url: URL to access the PDF.
• authors: list of authors.
An author comprises in nine features:
• name: string for the author name, as filled by the depositor.
• affiliations: list of unique identifiers attributed by HAL to the institutions where the author belongs.

• halauthorid: unique identifier assigned by HAL to each author registered on the online repository. If an author is not registered to HAL, he is considered unidentified and is assigned an halauthorid of "0".
• Potentially six external identifiers, if provided by the registered authors, comprising of arxiv, researcherid, idref, orcid, viaf and isni.

div.head: B.1 Language composition
div.p: See Tables 4 and5.

div.head: B.2 Domain composition
div.p: See Table 6.

div.head: C Experiments
div.p: We train all the models for 2 epochs, with a batch size of 128. For message passing, we sample 32 random neighbor nodes in the first hope and 16 nodes in the second hope. We chose those numbers because the average number of citations per article is 20. Thus, sampling 32 neighboring nodes from an author ↔ paper edge allows us to capture most of the cited papers as well as the authors. For each positive author ↔ paper, we sample two negative pairs for the model to train on, those pairs are randomly sampled at each step.
12 https://hal.science/browse/domain

div.head: C.1 Link prediction hyperparameters
div.p: We used the following hyperparameters for each moedl:
• GraphSage:
-Hidden channels: 64 -Dropout: 0.1 -Weight Decay: 1 × 10 -7 -Learning rate: 1 × 10 -3
• GAT:
-Hidden channels: 64 -Dropout: 0.5 -Number of attention heads: 8 -Weight Decay: 1 × 10 -4 -Learning rate: 5 × 10 -3
• RGGC:
-Hidden channels: 16
